name: Offline Personal Assistant (Hermes)
tagline: Hermes Agent running on a personal PC with a local LLM (qwen3.5:9b).
description: A desktop personal assistant that runs entirely on local hardware — no cloud API calls. Hermes handles tasks, remembers context in SQLite, and is driven by qwen3.5:9b through a React + Electron shell with a Python backend.
overview: Most AI assistants send every prompt to a remote API. Hermes goes the other way: a local qwen3.5:9b model on a personal PC, wrapped in an Electron desktop app with a Python backend and SQLite for persistent memory. The goal was a useful daily assistant that stays private, works offline, and costs nothing per query.

highlights.label: Problem
highlights.detail: Cloud assistants are convenient but leak context, need connectivity, and rack up API costs for always-on use.

highlights.label: Approach
highlights.detail: A Hermes agent loop backed by qwen3.5:9b locally, with YAML-configured tools, SQLite memory, and a React + Electron UI for day-to-day interaction.

highlights.label: Outcome
highlights.detail: A working offline assistant demo — private, self-hosted, and runnable entirely on personal hardware. Walkthrough on YouTube.

name: StatusMaxx
tagline: QREC grant winner · applying for YC Fall 2026.
description: StatusMaxx helps users discover credit card promotions matched to their wallet. Winner of the Kyushu University QREC entrepreneurship fund grant. Built with Expo/React Native (iOS & Android), a Python pipeline for daily promo extraction, and Supabase as the backend.
overview: Most people carry several cards but rarely know which one gives the best perk at the merchant in front of them. StatusMaxx closes that gap: it matches live card promotions to the cards a user actually holds, with proximity-based discovery so the right offer surfaces at the right place.

highlights.label: Problem
highlights.detail: Card perks are scattered across issuer pages and change constantly — value is left on the table because nobody tracks them per-merchant.

highlights.label: Approach
highlights.detail: A daily Python pipeline extracts and structures promotions, which are matched against each user's wallet and surfaced by proximity to nearby merchants.

highlights.label: Recognition
highlights.detail: Winner of the Kyushu University QREC entrepreneurship fund grant; currently applying for Y Combinator Fall 2026.

name: Sarungallo Holdings
tagline: Tracking a $3.5M family-office investment fund across four asset classes.
description: A self-hosted dashboard for a $3.5 million family-office fund spanning real estate, equities, gold, and crypto — valued in USD or IDR. Live price refresh writes timestamped snapshots so value-over-time graphs accumulate automatically. Features allocation donut, sector breakdown, top movers, and per-asset detail pages. Built with Next.js and Supabase.
overview: A self-hosted command center for a $3.5M family-office fund spread across real estate, equities, gold, and crypto. Every refresh writes a timestamped snapshot, so performance history accumulates on its own rather than depending on a third-party broker view.

highlights.label: Four asset classes, one view
highlights.detail: Real estate, equities, gold, and crypto valued together in either USD or IDR, with an allocation donut and sector breakdown.

highlights.label: History that builds itself
highlights.detail: Live price refresh writes timestamped snapshots, so value-over-time graphs accumulate automatically without manual logging.

highlights.label: Drill-down detail
highlights.detail: Top movers on the dashboard, plus per-asset detail pages for position-level context.

name: FinTrack
tagline: An experiment: can a locally-run LLM build a full-stack app?
description: Built first by a local Gemma model, then reviewed and hardened by Claude. Tracks income and expenses across IDR, USD, and JPY with daily FX conversion, trend charts, a category donut, and month/year history filters. Next.js + Supabase.
overview: A deliberate experiment: how far can a model running entirely on local hardware get on a real full-stack app? The first build was generated by a local Gemma model — running through LM Studio's local server and driven by OpenCode in build mode — then reviewed and hardened by Claude. The result is a working multi-currency expense tracker, and an honest look at where local models shine and where they stall.

highlights.label: The question
highlights.detail: Can a model running entirely on local hardware ship something real — not a toy — end to end?

highlights.label: The build
highlights.detail: Gemma ran locally via LM Studio (server at 127.0.0.1:1234), with OpenCode generating the Next.js app component by component: theme toggle, transaction form, smart categories, dark mode.

highlights.label: Where it broke
highlights.detail: Mid-build the app collapsed into a bare-HTML “recovery mode” — no styling, no charts. A vivid reminder that local models can scaffold fast but struggle to hold a real app together.

highlights.label: The fix
highlights.detail: Claude reviewed and hardened the codebase — restoring the UI, multi-currency logic, and charts — turning the experiment into something that actually runs.
